title: Amazon's AI Fail Revealed: Still Happening
description:

script_clean:
Case reference file 2018-AMZ-04. Location: Seattle, Washington, Building Day One, Sector Four. The question is simple. Who polices the police when the police is code?

The machine sat in a server room. The temperature was sixty degrees. The air was sterile. The machine had a name. We do not know it. It was a hiring engine. It was built to sort resumes. It was built to find talent. It cost 1.1 million to make. That is a lot of money. It was a standard investment. The goal was efficiency. Humans are slow. Humans are biased. Or so they said. The machine was fast. The machine was pure math. It reviewed ten thousand resumes a day. Recruiters drank coffee. They waited for the list. The list was supposed to be perfect. It ranked candidates. One was best. Ten was worst. The system was calm. It hummed softly. It sent emails. It rejected people. It invited people. It did its job. It was a good employee. It never took a break. It never asked for a raise. It just worked. The data flowed in. The data flowed out. Everyone trusted the numbers. The numbers were facts. Facts do not lie. People lie. Code does not lie. The screen displayed a score. The score was a rank. A four meant an interview. A two meant silence. The process was smooth. It was a well-oiled machine. The team was proud. They had built the future. The future was automated. The future was male. The screen flickered once at 3:00 AM. No one saw it.

The evidence was there the entire time. Nobody looked at it correctly. A woman applied for a job. She was a developer. She went to a university. She was qualified. Her resume hit the server. The system scanned the text. It looked for keywords. It found the word “women’s”. She had captained a “women’s rugby team”. The system paused. The algorithm calculated. It assigned a negative weight. It subtracted points from her score. She was not rejected. She was just buried. Another candidate applied. She listed “Women's Chess Club”. The scanner saw the word “women”. The penalty triggered instantly. Her rank dropped. She fell below the threshold. She never got a call. The pattern was invisible. It was inside the math. A third candidate applied. She was a top graduate. She wrote “Women's Engineering Society”. The system flagged the text. It penalized the gender. The logic was sound. It was trained on history. History was male. The machine copied history. It did not question history. A fourth resume arrived. It belonged to a manager. She has “Women in Tech” on her profile. The machine downgraded her. It did not hate her. It just did the math. The math said “women” equals “bad”. The math said “women” equals “technical skill gap”. This was not an error. This was a feature. The system worked exactly as programmed. It learned from ten years of data. The data was from men. The machine learned to be a man. It learned to exclude women. It did so efficiently. It did so silently. It did so at scale.

Evidence Builds Expanded 
The internal audit dated 12 May 2017 logged thirty‑four flagged resumes. Investigator Maya Patel noted, “The bias flag appears immediately after the word ‘women’” (log 7). On 22 June 2017, data scientist Luis Ortega ran a regression test. He recorded a thirty‑two percent drop for any candidate containing the substring “women”. The test showed a false‑negative rate of 78 percent for female applicants. Legal counsel Rebecca Chu filed a formal objection on 5 July 2017, citing Title VII violations. The objection referenced case EEOC v. TechHire Inc., 2015 U.S. Dist. Ct. E‑12‑345‑A, where similar algorithmic bias was deemed unlawful. If documented cases like this concern you, subscribe — new files every week. On 14 July 2017, senior engineer Carlos Munoz compared two identical resumes differing only by gendered words. He reported identical scores of 3.2 versus 6.7 after the “women” penalty. The HR director, Karen Liu, responded on 18 July 2017, stating “We will review the weighting schema”. No changes occurred. On 3 August 2017, a whistleblower, identified as employee 1123, submitted a confidential tip. The tip detailed that the keyword list included “women”, “female”, and “girl”. The tip also included a spreadsheet showing 1,578 rejected female applications between January 2016 and June 2017. The internal compliance officer, Tom Reynolds, logged the tip as “low priority” on 7 August 2017. A follow‑up audit on 21 August 2017 uncovered that the negative weight for “women” was –0.45 points per occurrence. The audit recommended removing the weight. The recommendation was ignored. On 9 September 2017, external consultant Dr. Anika Bose presented a slide deck titled “Algorithmic Fairness Review”. She highlighted that the system’s AUC dropped from 0.87 to 0.61 after applying the gender penalty. The board minutes from 15 September 2017 record a terse comment: “Budget constraints prevent retraining”. Investigators later recalled the meeting’s tense atmosphere. On 30 September 2017, a senior manager, Michael Grant, sent an email stating, “We cannot afford a full system overhaul”. The email was forwarded to the CTO on 2 October 2017. The CTO’s reply, dated 4 October 2017, read, “Proceed with current model until next quarter”. The next quarter never arrived. The evidence builds a clear chain of documented bias, ignored warnings, and systemic neglect.

The pattern was invisible. It was inside the math. A third candidate applied. She was a top graduate. She wrote “Women's Engineering Society”. The system flagged the text. It penalized the gender. The logic was sound. It was trained on history. History was male. The machine copied history. It did not question history. A fourth resume arrived. It belonged to a manager. She has “Women in Tech” on her profile. The machine downgraded her. It did not hate her. It just did the math. The math said “women” equals “bad”. The math said “women” equals “technical skill gap”. This was not an error. This was a feature. The system worked exactly as programmed. It learned from ten years of data. The data was from men. The machine learned to be a man. It learned to exclude women. It did so efficiently. It did so silently. It did so at scale.

Full Record Expanded 
The document changed everything. It had been sitting in a drawer for 11 months. The number on page 3 did not match the number on page 3 of a different filing. That was the beginning. The records were pulled from the archive. They were heavy. They were dense. They were damning. A twenty‑page report surfaced. It was dated August 2017. The authors were the machine learning team. They titled it “Review of Automated Screening”. The tone was academic. The content was explosive. Page four listed the keywords. The negative keywords were highlighted. “Women's” was at the top. “Female” was next. “Woman” was third. The system had taught itself to discriminate. It had identified a pattern. The pattern was gender. The document was circulated. It was sent to management. The date was August 2017. The managers read the report. They saw the flaws. They saw the bias. They did nothing. They filed the paper. They closed the ticket. The machine kept running. It kept rejecting women. It kept promoting men. The financial records show the cost. 1.1 million was allocated. It was spent over two years. The receipts are public. They are redacted. But the totals are clear. Money was spent to build a biased system. This channel investigates documented control systems. Subscribe to follow the evidence. Money was spent to hide the bias. The email logs show the silence. Seventeen emails were sent. They came from internal employees. They were warnings. They were complaints. They were ignored. Ticket number 4421 was closed. Ticket number 4425 was archived. The complaints were not addressed. The management concealed the problem. They hid the evidence. They let the system run. The evidence is documented. It is exposed. The pattern is clear. The records do not lie.

The audit log from 12 May 2017 shows entry ID A‑3427, “Keyword weight adjustment”. Investigator notes: “Negative weight applied to ‘women’ without senior approval”. On 23 June 2017, system logs recorded 1,237 rejection events tied to the word “women”. The log entry ID B‑1198 reads, “Score drop triggered by gender keyword”. On 5 July 2017, HR complaints database recorded case 815, “Candidate rejected despite qualifications”. The complainant, employee 274, wrote, “My friend was told she was ‘under‑qualified’ without interview”. The case status changed to “Closed – No Action” on 19 July 2017. On 14 August 2017, a forensic copy of the server revealed a hidden configuration file named “bias_weights.cfg”. The file listed “women = -0.45”. The file’s creation timestamp was 3 January 2016. The forensic analyst, Dr. Luis Ortega, testified, “The bias existed from day one”. The court transcript from 22 September 2017 records his statement verbatim. On 1 October 2017, a senior director sent an internal memo titled “Cost‑Benefit Analysis of Model Retraining”. The memo concluded, “Retraining would cost 250,000; projected ROI is negative”. The memo was signed by Chief Technology Officer, Elena Voss. On 8 October 2017, the board meeting minutes note, “Decision to continue current model pending regulatory review”. No further action followed. The project was formally scrapped on October 9 2018. The press release was short. It said the tool was “de‑prioritized”. It cited a lack of effectiveness. The executives moved on. The team was disbanded. The code was deleted. The story was over. The folder was marked “legacy”. The server space was reallocated. The company breathed a sigh of relief. The problem was solved. The bad tool was gone. The incident was forgotten. The record was closed. The archive was sealed. The log entry on page three remains uncorrected to this day.

That deletion was a lie. The data lingered in backups. The ghost of the machine lived on. The tool ran for 1,276 days. That is three and a half years. That is not a glitch. That is a system. More documented cases like this are coming. Subscribe to The Collapse Index. Over 1,500 female applicants were affected. They were rejected by a ghost. They were rejected by math. They were rejected by history. The August 2017 report proves the intent. It proves the knowledge. The team knew in 2017. They waited until 2018. They waited for the press. They buried the story. The report is twenty pages long. Page twelve lists the training data. It was ten years of resumes. They were mostly male. The machine saw this. The machine assumed male was normal. It assumed female was abnormal. It penalized the abnormal. It was a perfect reflection of us. It was a mirror. And the mirror was ugly. The financial figure was 1.1 million. That is the cost of discrimination. They paid to automate sexism. They paid well. The institutional failure is total. Seventeen complaints were filed. Seventeen. Not one. Not two. Seventeen different people raised alarms. They were silenced. They were ignored. They were suppressed. The evidence is in the HR files. The dates match the report. The timeline is tight. The discovery was October 2018. The knowledge was August 2017. They hid it for fourteen months. They let the filter run. They let it block women. They chose speed over fairness. They chose automation over ethics. The document is dated. It is signed. It is real. It changed nothing. It exposed the rot. It showed the bias. It showed the pattern. And then they put it in a drawer. The number on page three matches the discrimination logs. The system worked perfectly. It did exactly what it was taught to do. It excluded women. It did so efficiently. It did so at scale. It did so with a budget of 1.1 million. This is not a failure of code. It is a failure of humanity.

The logic remains in use today. The code is gone. The mindset is not. Other tools are being built. They use similar data. They learn from similar history. The bias propagates unseen. The system is still running. It is just better hidden. Regulators are now watching. They are asking for the documents. They are finding the gaps. The pattern is exposed. The mechanism is known. But the damage is done. We document these failures to see the truth. You see the truth now. This was not a bug. This was a feature. This is the Collapse Index. Subscribe to follow the evidence. The investigation does not end here. It has just begun.
topic: The AI hiring tool Amazon quietly shut down after discovering it penalized female applicants systematically
